title: Everything you need to understand the platform lives here.
subtitle: Use this guide to move from the big-picture architecture into service detail, UI capability, security boundaries, operating workflows, and deeper reference material.
bullets: Platform: what ships in the stack, why each service exists, and how the user, data, and control paths fit together.
Services: Watchdog, Gatekeeper, Notifier, and Resolver are broken out separately so each backend has a clear responsibility model.
UI: every major operator page is grouped by job-to-be-done instead of being buried in one generic summary.
Security and flows: authentication, tenant scoping, OTLP validation, alert routing, Grafana bootstrap, and RCA execution are described as end-to-end paths.
Reference links: architecture, deployment, and operator guides are available directly from this section whenever you want the raw source material.

title: Watchdog is one product built from application services plus observability infrastructure.
subtitle: The platform combines a control plane, ingestion security, alerting workflows, RCA, and the LGTM runtime into one operating model.

rows: Auth, users, groups, API keys, secure proxying, health, readiness, and UI-facing workflows live here.
Validates x-otlp-token, enforces allowlists and rate limits, and resolves tenant scope for telemetry ingest.
Runs asynchronous jobs across logs, metrics, and traces to produce ranked root-cause evidence.
Owns rules, channels, silences, incidents, webhook ingestion, and Jira integration flows.
The place operators actually work: logs, traces, dashboards, alerts, incidents, API keys, users, audit, and RCA.
Receives OTLP traffic and calls Gatekeeper before forwarding to Loki, Tempo, and Mimir.
Keeps Grafana behind Watchdog-authenticated and RBAC-enforced access instead of exposing it directly.
Persistent application data, token/cache state, rate limiting, and shared fast state.
Storage, query, rule evaluation, alert routing, and visualization backends under the product layer.

title: Users, telemetry, alerts, and RCA each follow a clear path through the platform.

workflowSteps.label: User path
workflowSteps.detail: The UI authenticates against Watchdog, receives identity plus permission context, and then calls Watchdog APIs for logs, traces, alerts, Grafana actions, API keys, users, and RCA.

workflowSteps.label: Telemetry path
workflowSteps.detail: Collectors send OTLP to Envoy on 4320, Envoy calls Gatekeeper, Gatekeeper validates x-otlp-token and returns X-Scope-OrgID, then Loki, Tempo, and Mimir ingest data under the right tenant.

workflowSteps.label: Alert path
workflowSteps.detail: Operators create rules through the UI, Watchdog forwards those actions to Notifier, Notifier synchronizes rule definitions to Mimir, Alertmanager routes notifications, and webhook events flow back into incidents.

workflowSteps.label: Grafana path
workflowSteps.detail: The UI requests a bootstrap session from Watchdog, the proxy sets the access cookie, and every sensitive /grafana route is authorized before the browser reaches Grafana.

workflowSteps.label: RCA path
workflowSteps.detail: The UI creates a job, Watchdog signs and forwards it to Resolver, Resolver queries logs, metrics, and traces, stores results, and returns structured reports back to the UI.

title: Watchdog is the control plane, security layer, and orchestration API.
bullets: Authentication and identity: local auth, password flow, MFA support, OIDC callback handling, bootstrap admin, user sessions, and permission-aware access checks are anchored here.
Access management: users, groups, API keys, inherited permissions, scoped actions, and the active tenant context used by downstream observability requests are all mediated here.
Observability proxy surface: logs, traces, alerting, Grafana hand-off, RCA job creation, system health, and internal validation APIs all hang off the main FastAPI server.
Security and control middleware: CORS, security headers, request-size limits, concurrency limits, validation handlers, and audit-oriented action logging are registered centrally.
Operational endpoints: health, readiness, docs, and internal OTLP validation give the rest of the platform a stable contract to depend on.

title: Gatekeeper makes OTLP ingest safe, tenant-aware, and hard to bypass.
bullets: It is called by Envoy as an external authorization service before telemetry is forwarded to Loki, Tempo, or Mimir.
It validates x-otlp-token, applies optional IP allowlists, applies request rate limits, and caches validation responses in memory or Redis.
On a cache miss it asks Watchdog to resolve the token, then returns the tenant scope to Envoy as X-Scope-OrgID so the backends ingest under the correct organization.
This means applications never need direct network access to Loki, Tempo, or Mimir, and tenant isolation starts at the ingestion edge instead of as an afterthought.
The gateway is what turns self-hosted LGTM into a protected multi-tenant ingest surface rather than an open collector endpoint.

title: Notifier turns raw alert delivery into alerting operations and incident management.
bullets: Rules: create, edit, import from YAML, dry-run preview, target the right scope, and synchronize definitions into Mimir for evaluation.
Channels: manage webhook, email, Slack, Teams, PagerDuty, and related notification targets with visibility controls and encrypted configuration.
Silences and active alerts: operators can inspect, create, and manage muting without hand-editing backend YAML or switching tools.
Incidents: alerts can produce incidents with assignment, notes, status changes, timeline history, and collaboration data in one workflow surface.
Jira and webhook handling: inbound Alertmanager webhooks feed the incident model, while Jira integration lets the platform sync downstream ticket state and comments.

title: Resolver is the analysis engine behind RCA, anomaly, topology, and forecast views.
bullets: Jobs and reports: it creates asynchronous RCA jobs, lists and retrieves them, stores saved reports, and exposes finished output for later review.
Multi-signal analysis: it reads from Loki, Tempo, and Mimir rather than duplicating them, combining logs, traces, and metrics into one ranked explanation model.
Evidence surfaces: summary, root causes, anomaly panels, clusters, topology, causal views, forecast, SLO, and warnings all come from the report model the UI renders.
Quality tuning: correlation windows, density limits, corroboration requirements, and confidence thresholds let operators tune how strict or permissive RCA should be.
Tenant-safe proxying: Watchdog forwards calls with service-to-service auth and scoped context so Resolver can analyze the correct tenant without becoming publicly exposed.

title: The UI is organized around operator jobs, not generic data-source tabs.
subtitle: The route surface in the frontend is permission-gated. Operators only see pages their current account can actually use.

rows: Health cards, activity widgets, service status, and reorderable overview metrics.
Builder-mode filters, labels, quick filters, raw LogQL, log volume views, and saved page state.
Trace search, service discovery, operation filters, direct trace lookup, timeline, and service graph exploration.
Job composer, queue, report lookup, summary, root causes, anomalies, clusters, topology, causal, forecast/SLO, and warnings.
Active alerts, rule CRUD, import preview, rule testing, silences, and hidden/shared object handling.
Incident board, assignment, notes, status changes, and Jira-linked operational workflow.
Dashboards, folders, datasources, and controlled hand-off into Grafana through the proxy session.
Channel configuration, sharing/visibility, Jira integrations, auth mode, and connectivity tests.
Create keys, set active scope, regenerate OTLP tokens, share with users/groups, and download starter collector config.
Identity management, group-based permissions, account lifecycle, audit filtering, and CSV export.

title: Observability pages cover the daily technical workflow from raw data to guided diagnosis.
bullets: Dashboard is the landing surface for platform health and activity, giving operators a quick answer to whether the stack itself is healthy before deeper investigation starts.
Logs is designed for both exploratory and precise work: label discovery, quick filters, raw queries, and volume context help operators move from broad search to exact evidence.
Traces focuses on search plus structure: service filters, operation filters, trace lookup, span tree inspection, duration/status controls, and graph views make request-path analysis faster.
Grafana is not a blind external link. It is a managed entry point for dashboards, folders, and datasources with Watchdog still controlling access.
RCA is the analysis cockpit where jobs are created, results are revisited, and different report facets are separated into summary, anomalies, topology, causal, and forecast-specific views.

title: Operational pages cover alerting, incidents, integrations, scope, and governance.
bullets: Alert Manager is where rule lifecycle work happens: create rules, import YAML safely, inspect active alerts, manage silences, and verify expressions before relying on them.
Incidents is the operations board: assignment, notes, statuses, active-alert linkage, and ticket synchronization turn alerts into coordinated work instead of chat fragments.
Integrations owns notification and Jira connectivity, including visibility boundaries so personal, team, and tenant-shared connectors stay explicit.
API Keys is one of the most important pages in the product because scope selection, OTLP token handling, collector generation, and shared access all converge there.
Users, Groups, and Audit provide the administrative control layer needed to move from a single-admin demo to a shared, governed platform with traceable changes.

title: The platform has three security boundaries that work together.

workflowSteps.label: User-to-application auth
workflowSteps.detail: Watchdog handles login, sessions, MFA, permissions, users, groups, and API-key-backed working scope for the UI and browser users.

workflowSteps.label: Telemetry ingest auth
workflowSteps.detail: Gatekeeper validates x-otlp-token before Envoy forwards data to Loki, Tempo, or Mimir so ingest is protected separately from browser auth.

workflowSteps.label: Service-to-service auth
workflowSteps.detail: Watchdog talks to Notifier and Resolver using dedicated service tokens and signed context so downstream services do not trust arbitrary callers.

workflowSteps.label: Tenant propagation
workflowSteps.detail: The active API key and request context determine X-Scope-OrgID and downstream scope, which is how the product preserves separation between organizations and products.

title: When you need exact behavior, these are the sources to trust first.
bullets: First trust docker-compose.yml because it defines the live topology, port exposure, and service wiring for this workspace.
Then trust .env.example because it is the configuration contract across Watchdog, Gatekeeper, Notifier, Resolver, and the supporting infrastructure.
Then trust service code such as main.py, routers, config modules, and frontend route/API files because they define the actual behavior exposed to users.
The README is the architecture overview, and USER_GUIDE is the operator runbook for install, first-run flows, page-by-page usage, and hardening.
